method_atom: an empirical attribution analysis that cross-correlates a high-frequency time series of a fluctuating signal with feeds of exogenous events to test whether external inputs explain the frequency and amplitude of large discontinuous jumps, and compares the conditional response profiles (amplitude and relaxation shape of the following fluctuations) triggered by exogenous events versus those of endogenous jumps; finding that jumps and external inputs are followed by oppositely-signed and differently-shaped fluctuation relaxations, and that jump size is uncorrelated with the magnitude of concurrent throughput, it infers that large discontinuities arise from endogenous flux fluctuations near a point of vanishing carrying capacity rather than from exogenous shocks.
object_atom: jumps in stock prices in financial markets, examined against idiosyncratic and market-wide news feeds, transaction volumes, and order flow near vanishing liquidity.
brief_reason: The transferable part is the exogenous-vs-endogenous attribution via cross-correlation and conditional response/relaxation profiling of a jumpy signal near a saturation threshold; the domain-specific object is stock prices driven by news, volume, and order-flow liquidity.
